# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 99

This is turn 99. Turns 1 through 98 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 99.

## Turn 99 — Thompson criticality is unnecessary

The \(A_2\) submission remains unchanged:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

The tame relation is implicit, and the exponent represents the genuine profinite idempotent \(\omega_2\); the displayed integer is only its verifier residue. 

Turn 98 left ordinary Thompson criticality as the sole external finite-group input.  It can be removed by a direct argument tailored to the minimal active block.

### Frattini–centralizer collapse lemma

Retain the fixed-frame notation. Let \(L\triangleleft G\) be the wild \(2\)-kernel, and choose
\[
S\triangleleft P\triangleleft G,\qquad P/S\simeq V,
\]
where every chief factor below \(S\) is scalar and \(V\) is the first nontrivial simple factor. Let
\[
K\triangleleft G,\qquad K\le P,\qquad KS=P
\]
be minimal with these properties.

Let \(N\) be the odd kernel of the maximal \(2\)-quotient of the tame quotient, and choose an odd Hall lift \(\widetilde N\) in \(G\).

Set
\[
R=\Phi(K).
\]

Then
\[
R\le Z(K),\qquad K^4=1,
\]
and consequently
\[
R=K^2[K,K]
\]
is elementary abelian.

#### 1. The odd tame subgroup centralizes the scalar run

Choose a \(G\)-chief series
\[
1=S_0\triangleleft S_1\triangleleft\cdots\triangleleft S_m=S.
\]
Since every \(S_i/S_{i-1}\) is trivial,
\[
[S_i,\widetilde N]\le S_{i-1}.
\]
Hence \([S,{}_m\widetilde N]=1\). Coprime action gives
\[
[S,\widetilde N]=[S,\widetilde N,\widetilde N]
               =\cdots
               =[S,{}_m\widetilde N]=1.
\tag{99.1}
\]

Because \(K/(K\cap S)\simeq V\) is elementary abelian,
\[
R=\Phi(K)\le K\cap S.
\]
Thus \(\widetilde N\) centralizes \(R\) pointwise.

#### 2. The Frattini subgroup is central

Put
\[
C=C_K(R).
\]
Because \(R\) is characteristic in \(K\), the subgroup \(C\) is characteristic in \(K\), hence normal in \(G\).

Since \([R,\widetilde N]=1\) and \([R,K]\le R\), the three-subgroup lemma gives
\[
[K,\widetilde N]\le C_K(R)=C.
\tag{99.2}
\]

The group \(N\) acts nontrivially on \(V\). Otherwise the action on \(V\) would factor through the \(2\)-group \(H/N\), whose only simple characteristic-two module is trivial. Since \(N\triangleleft H\) and \(V\) is simple,
\[
[V,N]=V.
\tag{99.3}
\]
Therefore
\[
[K,\widetilde N]S=P.
\]
By (99.2), \(CS=P\). Minimality of \(K\) now forces
\[
C=K.
\]
Hence
\[
R\le Z(K).
\tag{99.4}
\]

In particular, \(K\) has nilpotency class at most two.

#### 3. The fourth-power map vanishes

Because \(K/R\) is elementary abelian, \(x^2\in R\le Z(K)\) for every \(x\in K\). Thus
\[
[x,y]^2=[x^2,y]=1.
\]
Consequently the fourth-power map
\[
f:K\longrightarrow R,\qquad f(x)=x^4
\]
is a homomorphism: in this class-two group,
\[
(xy)^4=x^4y^4.
\tag{99.5}
\]

It is also \(G\)-equivariant. Since \(\widetilde N\) fixes \(R\) pointwise,
\[
f([k,n])=f(k)^{-1}f(k^n)
        =f(k)^{-1}f(k)^n
        =1
\]
for \(k\in K\) and \(n\in\widetilde N\).

Let
\[
J=\langle [K,\widetilde N]^G\rangle.
\]
Equation (99.3) gives \(JS=P\); minimality of \(K\) therefore gives \(J=K\). Equivariance of \(f\) shows that \(f\) vanishes on every \(G\)-conjugate of \([K,\widetilde N]\), hence on \(J=K\). Therefore
\[
K^4=1.
\tag{99.6}
\]

Now \(K^2\) and \([K,K]\) consist of central involutions, so
\[
R=\Phi(K)=K^2[K,K]
\]
is central elementary abelian.

### Consequence for the Turn-98 proof

This argument replaces Input T completely. It also avoids the precise error found in Turn 79: it never asserts that \([K,\widetilde N]\) itself is \(G\)-normal. Instead it uses:

- the characteristic subgroup \(C_K(\Phi(K))\) to obtain centrality;
- the \(G\)-normal closure of \([K,\widetilde N]\) to kill the fourth-power map.

Thus the fixed-frame proof no longer depends on Thompson criticality or on a missing bibliography. Its only substantive standard input is now the dyadic Kummer/Hilbert package used to identify the local determinant zero counts.

**ESTABLISHED**: Eliminated ordinary Thompson criticality from the proof. Proved directly that the minimal active \(2\)-subgroup \(K\) has class at most two and exponent at most four, with \(\Phi(K)\) central elementary abelian. This supplies exactly the critical block required by the finite-Heisenberg, Fourier, and determinant arguments.

**GAP**: The private 5402-group verifier remains unrun. The remaining external mathematical package is the standard dyadic Kummer/Hilbert filtration used on the local side.

**NEXT**: Replace Input T in Turn 98 by the Frattini–centralizer collapse lemma above and produce the one-input standalone proof note with the unchanged submission block.

[BREAKTHROUGH]

Make the most efficient, compact verified we can make against all the known predicted values we have from Pro that we can parallelize across the compute cluster across however many CPUs we deem fit (one has 128, one has 256). Make it be efficient from many standpoints, including mathematically as well as computationally (using efficient libraries and perhaps a language C), without sacrificing accuracy.
